request.question: Which war was being fought when C.S. Lewis wrote the book whose chapter shares a title with the band Wild Sweet Orange's 2008 album?

title: The Whale EP
We Have Cause to Be Uneasy
The Runaway Found
The Company They Keep
The Personal Heresy
Out of the Silent Planet (album)
Wild Sweet Orange
Max McLean
Mere Christianity
Letters to Malcolm
sentences: The Whale EP is a self-released EP by Wild Sweet Orange.
It contains 5 songs and has a running time of 20 minutes and 20 seconds.
We Have Cause to Be Uneasy is the first and final full-length album by American indie rock band Wild Sweet Orange and was released on July 29, 2008, available on CD, Vinyl, and via iTunes.
"We Have Cause to Be Uneasy" is the title of the fifth chapter in C.S. Lewis' book, "Mere Christianity".
The Runaway Found is The Veils debut album, released on 16 February 2004.
The record was preceded by several singles, from "More Heat Than Light" (November 2002) to "The Wild Son" (January 2004).
The last single "Lavinia" was released in November 2003, alongside a first promotional version of the album which featured a different running order and track list.
In fact "...& One of Us Must Go" and "Wires to Flying Birds" did not make the final version and were later replaced by "The Wild Son" and "The Valleys of New Orleans".

Because of its long gestation (the recording sessions started straight after the band's first deal with Blanco Y Negro in 2001) the record has three different producers: Matthew Ollivier (tracks 3, 4, 6, 9, 10), Bernard Butler (tracks 1, 2, 5, 8) and Ken Nelson (track 7).
The fourth song on the set, "More Heat Than Light", gives credits to Finn's father Barry Andrews as co-author.
According to the booklet notes the last song of the set, "The Nowhere Man", is taken from the 1954 novel by C.S. Lewis "The Horse and His Boy".
The song "Vicious Traditions" was featured during the end credits of the 2007 film "Mr. Brooks".

The Company They Keep: C. S. Lewis and J. R. R. Tolkien as Writers in Community (2007) is a non-fiction book written by Diana Pavlac Glyer, an Inklings scholar and English professor at Azusa Pacific University.

"The Company They Keep" challenges the commonly held belief that the Inklings did not influence each other through a detailed and engaging examination of both published and unpublished works, papers, and letters written by J.R.R. Tolkien, C.S. Lewis, Charles Williams, Owen Barfield, Warren Lewis and the lesser-known writers who comprised the Inklings.

The Personal Heresy is a series of articles, three each by C.S. Lewis and E. M. W. (Eustace Mandeville Wetenhall) Tillyard, first published on 27 April 1939 by Oxford University Press and later reprinted, also by Oxford University Press, in 1965.
The book has been reprinted in 2008 by Concordia University Press with an Introduction by Lewis scholar Bruce L. Edwards and a new Preface by the editor, Joel D. Heck.

The central issue of the essays is whether a piece of imaginative writing, particularly poetry, is primarily a reflection of the author's personality (Tillyard's position) or is about something external to the author (Lewis's position).
The two positions may be summarized briefly as the subjective position (Tillyard) and the objective position (Lewis).
In general, Lewis attempts to keep poetry within the reach of the common person, while Tillyard thinks of the poet as a person who is "a cut above the common person."
Out of the Silent Planet is the debut release from King's X.
The title of the album comes from that of a book by C.S. Lewis, an author favored by band members Ty Tabor and Jerry Gaskill.
"Out of the Silent Planet" is also the title of the first track from the follow up album "Gretchen Goes to Nebraska".
The cover art features the skyline of Houston with the southern out line of the state of Texas.
Wild Sweet Orange was an American indie rock band from Homewood, Alabama.
In early 2008 their song "Land of No Return" was featured on Grey's Anatomy.
The band performed the song "Ten Dead Dogs" live on the Late Show with David Letterman on June 23, 2008.
Their first full LP, "We Have Cause to Be Uneasy", was released on July 29, 2008.
Wild Sweet Orange has opened for bands such as Counting Crows, Guster, Augustana, and Gringo Star.
Max McLean is the founder and artistic director of Fellowship for Performing Arts, a New York City-based producer of live theater from a Christian worldview.
McLean conceived, adapted, produced and starred in "The Screwtape Letters", a play based on the book by Oxford and Cambridge scholar, author and fantasy writer C.S. Lewis.
His stage adaptation of Lewis’ "The Great Divorce" launched its national tour in late 2013.
McLean adapted, co-directed and starred in "C.S. Lewis Onstage: The Most Reluctant Convert", and co-wrote and produced "Martin Luther on Trial".
McLean also is the narrator of The Listener's Bible.
Mere Christianity is a theological book by C. S. Lewis, adapted from a series of BBC radio talks made between 1941 and 1944, while Lewis was at Oxford during the Second World War.

Considered a classic of Christian apologetics, the transcripts of the broadcasts originally appeared in print as three separate pamphlets: "The Case for Christianity" ("Broadcast Talks" in the UK) (1942), "Christian Behaviour" (1943), and "Beyond Personality" (1944).
Lewis was invited to give the talks by the Rev. James Welch, the BBC Director of Religious Broadcasting, who had read his 1940 book, "The Problem of Pain".
Letters to Malcolm: Chiefly on Prayer is a book by C.S. Lewis posthumously published in 1964.
The book takes the form of a series of letters to a fictional friend, "Malcolm", in which Lewis meditates on prayer as an intimate dialogue between man and God.

Beginning with a discussion of "corporate prayer" and the liturgical service, Lewis goes on to consider practical and metaphysical aspects of private prayer, such as when to pray and where, ready-made prayer, petitionary prayer, prayer as worship, penitential prayer, and prayer for the dead.
The concluding letter discusses "liberal" Christians, the soul and resurrection.

title: We Have Cause to Be Uneasy
We Have Cause to Be Uneasy
Mere Christianity

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: Which war was being fought when C.S. Lewis wrote the book whose chapter shares a title with the band Wild Sweet Orange's 2008 album?
hop1_docs: We Have Cause to Be Uneasy | We Have Cause to Be Uneasy is the first and final full-length album by American indie rock band Wild Sweet Orange and was released on July 29, 2008, available on CD, Vinyl, and via iTunes.  "We Have Cause to Be Uneasy" is the title of the fifth chapter in C.S. Lewis' book, "Mere Christianity".

Wild Sweet Orange | Wild Sweet Orange was an American indie rock band from Homewood, Alabama.  In early 2008 their song "Land of No Return" was featured on Grey's Anatomy.  The band performed the song "Ten Dead Dogs" live on the Late Show with David Letterman on June 23, 2008.  Their first full LP, "We Have Cause to Be Uneasy", was released on July 29, 2008.  Wild Sweet Orange has opened for bands such as Counting Crows, Guster, Augustana, and Gringo Star.

Guillotine IV (The Final Chapter) | 'Guillotine IV (The Final Chapter)' is the second single from Falling in Reverse's third album "Just Like You".  It is the fourth and final installment of the Guillotine series, which was started by Escape the Fate when former lead singer Ronnie Radke was in the band.  The first song titled 'The Guillotine' was in Escape the Fate's debut album Dying Is Your Latest Fashion in 2006.  The second song which was titled 'This War Is Ours (The Guillotine II)' was on Escape the Fate's second studio album This War Is Ours in 2008.  The third song which was titled 'The Aftermath (The Guillotine III)' was on Escape the Fate's third self-titled album in 2010.  Then finally in 2015 Falling in Reverse finished the series with ""Guillotine IV (The Final Chapter).

On being asked for a War Poem | "On being asked for a War Poem" is a poem by William Butler Yeats written on February 6, 1915 in response to a request by Henry James that Yeats compose a political poem about World War I.  Yeats changed the poem's title from "To a friend who has asked me to sign his manifesto to the neutral nations" to "A Reason for Keeping Silent" before sending it in a letter to James, which Yeats wrote at Coole Park on August 20, 1915.  The poem was prefaced with a note stating: "It is the only thing I have written of the war or will write, so I hope it may not seem unfitting."  The poem was first published in Edith Wharton's "The Book of the Homeless" in 1916 as "A Reason for Keeping Silent".  When it was later reprinted in "The Wild Swans at Coole", the title was changed to "On being asked for a War Poem".

Orange Island | Orange Island was a rock band based out of the town Clinton, Massachusetts.  Orange Island formed in 1996 when cousins Charles Young and Brendan Dickhaut started writing music together and had David Chouinard playing bass guitar.  After writing songs together they asked their friend David Gorman to sing for the group.  Orange Island wrote and recorded several demos before being signed to the Boston-based record label Iodine Recordings in 2000. " The Shape of Calling" was Orange Island's debut album and shortly after its release the band started touring the US with many well known bands including Brand New, The Movielife, Breaking Pangaea, Kill Verona and others.  in 2002 Orange Island released their follow-up full length titled "Everything You Thought You Knew".  This album dealt with themes related to struggling with alcoholism and losing a close friend.

The Joker Is Wild (album) | The Joker is Wild was the second album released by Alex Harvey after the demise of The Soul Band.  The album was released in 1972.  Some time after 1972 the album "The Joker Is Wild" was reissued and repackaged, the album song listings stayed the same, but the album was credited as being made by "The Sensational Alex Harvey Band" even though this band did not exist at the time, and the title was changed to "This Is".

List of songs recorded by She & Him | She & Him is an American musical duo consisting of members Zooey Deschanel and M. Ward.  They have recorded songs for six studio albums and an extended play (EP), as well as various other releases.  The two formed the band after working on their rendition of Richard and Linda Thompson's "When I Get to the Border", which included on the soundtrack of Martin Hynes's 2007 film "The Go-Getter" and was used during its closing credits.  Described as "a collection of vintage-sounding country-tinged songs", their debut album "Volume One", released under indie label Merge Records in 2008.  Deschanel wrote all of the album's original tracks, except "Sweet Darlin'", which she co-wrote with Jason Schwartzman.

List of songs recorded by She & Him

- "We Have Cause to Be Uneasy" — [1] quote: "July 29, 2008"

- "C.S. Lewis" — [1] quote: "C.S. Lewis' book"
- "Wild Sweet Orange" — [2] quote: "Wild Sweet Orange was an American indie rock band"

Ambiguities:
- None of the passages present multiple candidates for "Mere Christianity" or "C.S. Lewis"; no conflicting dates or variants are given in the supplied passages.

Bridge / missing fact:
- The passages do NOT state when "Mere Christianity" was written or first published, nor do they state which war (e.g., "World War I" or "World War II") was being fought at that time. The relation needed: "war being fought when C.S. Lewis wrote Mere Christianity" is missing.

Retrieval direction:
- Open page: "Mere Christianity" (page about the book)
- Open page: "C. S. Lewis" (biography page)
- Search query: "When was Mere Christianity written" (quoted)
- Search query: "\"Mere Christianity\" radio talks 1941 1944 World War II" (quoted)

Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets above.
hop2_docs: Mere Christianity | Mere Christianity is a theological book by C. S. Lewis, adapted from a series of BBC radio talks made between 1941 and 1944, while Lewis was at Oxford during the Second World War.  Considered a classic of Christian apologetics, the transcripts of the broadcasts originally appeared in print as three separate pamphlets: "The Case for Christianity" ("Broadcast Talks" in the UK) (1942), "Christian Behaviour" (1943), and "Beyond Personality" (1944).  Lewis was invited to give the talks by the Rev. James Welch, the BBC Director of Religious Broadcasting, who had read his 1940 book, "The Problem of Pain".

Between Heaven and Hell (novel) | Between Heaven and Hell: A Dialog Somewhere Beyond Death with John F. Kennedy, C. S. Lewis, & Aldous Huxley is a novel by Peter Kreeft about U.S. President John F. Kennedy, and authors C. S. Lewis ("The Chronicles of Narnia") and Aldous Huxley ("Brave New World") meeting in Purgatory and engaging in a philosophical discussion on faith.  It was inspired by the fact that all three men died on the same day: November 22, 1963.  We see from the three points of view: Kennedy's "modern Christian" view, Lewis's "conservative Christian" or "mere Christian" view, and Huxley's "Orientalized Christian" view.  The book progresses as Lewis and Kennedy discuss Jesus' being God incarnate, to Lewis and Huxley discussing whether or not Jesus was a deity or "just a good person."

C. S. Lewis | Clive Staples Lewis (29 November 1898 – 22 November 1963) was a British novelist, poet, academic, medievalist, literary critic, essayist, lay theologian, broadcaster, lecturer, and Christian apologist.  He held academic positions at both Oxford University (Magdalen College, 1925–1954) and Cambridge University (Magdalene College, 1954–1963).  He is best known for his works of fiction, especially "The Screwtape Letters", "The Chronicles of Narnia", and "The Space Trilogy", and for his non-fiction Christian apologetics, such as "Mere Christianity", "Miracles", and "The Problem of Pain".

Robert Lewis Dabney | Robert Lewis Dabney (March 5, 1820 – January 3, 1898) was an American Christian theologian, Southern Presbyterian pastor, Confederate States Army chaplain, and architect.  He was also chief of staff and biographer to Stonewall Jackson.  His biography of Jackson remains in print today.

W. E. B. Du Bois: Biography of a Race, 1868–1919 | W.E.B. Du Bois: Biography of a Race, 1868–1919 was written by historian David Levering Lewis and published in 1994 by Henry Holt and Company.  The book studies the early and middle years of Du Bois's life.  It is the first in a two-part biography of W.E.B. Du Bois.  It won the Pulitzer Prize for Biography in 1994, as did Lewis's second installment, "", winning the Pulitzer in 2001.

Julia Namier | Lady Julia Namier (also known as Iulia de Beausobre, née Iulia Michaelovna Kazarina) (1893-1977) was a Russian writer, who married the British historian Lewis Bernstein Namier.  She wrote several works on Christian spirituality, and a biography of her husband.

We Have Cause to Be Uneasy | We Have Cause to Be Uneasy is the first and final full-length album by American indie rock band Wild Sweet Orange and was released on July 29, 2008, available on CD, Vinyl, and via iTunes.  "We Have Cause to Be Uneasy" is the title of the fifth chapter in C.S. Lewis' book, "Mere Christianity".

We Have Cause to Be Uneasy
